# Brain health and dementia

> *A 52-year-old whose mother has dementia sits down and asks the question you will hear again and
> again: "Doctor, what should I eat so I don't get it too?" The real answer is not a superfood or a
> supplement. It starts with the blood vessels.*

## Brain health at a glance

Dementia is the condition patients most want a food fix for, and careful counselling matters here,
because the evidence is genuinely encouraging in one place and genuinely thin in the others. Four
messages carry it:

- **The biggest dietary lever on the brain is vascular.** What protects the heart protects the brain.
  Managing blood pressure, glucose, weight, lipids, smoking and activity is the best-supported,
  most actionable advice, and it is the same prevention taught in the *Cardiovascular Disease &
  Hypertension* and *Type 2 Diabetes* chapters.
- **A brain-healthy eating pattern is a reasonable, low-regret choice, not a proven cure.** The
  cohort studies look impressive; the one prevention trial built to test a brain diet was negative.
- **Brain supplements are the clearest "save your money" advice in the book.** None is proven to
  prevent dementia, and a few can do harm.
- **Late in the disease the problem inverts.** Weight loss and difficulty eating are an expected part
  of advanced dementia, not a nutritional gap to fill with a feeding tube.

Throughout, the altitude is to **recognise the levers, counsel truthfully, and refer**. There are no
drug names or doses to memorise here.

## Two pathways, one brain

Dementia is a clinical syndrome of acquired, progressive loss of memory and thinking severe enough to
affect daily life. Underneath it, two kinds of damage usually run together.

The first is **neurodegeneration**: the accumulation of abnormal proteins (amyloid-β in plaques
between neurons, and tau in tangles inside them), with loss of synapses and neurons and shrinkage of
the brain. This is the dominant picture in Alzheimer's disease. The exact causal chain is still
debated, so treat it as the leading model, not settled fact.

The second is **vascular**: damage to the brain's small blood vessels, **cerebral small-vessel
disease**. It extends the *Cardiovascular Disease & Hypertension* chapter's story, where chronically
raised blood pressure damages blood vessels and drives stroke, to the brain's own small vessels.
Chronic high pressure stiffens and thickens small arterioles, which impairs blood flow and injures the
surrounding white matter, producing the lacunar strokes (small deep infarcts) and white-matter changes
seen on scans. **Diabetes adds to the same vascular injury**: the high-glucose, insulin-resistant state
described in the *Type 2 Diabetes* chapter also damages the brain's small vessels and speeds up brain
shrinkage [@biessels2014diabetesmri]. There is no separate "diabetic dementia".

![Two kinds of damage usually combine to produce dementia. Neurodegeneration (amyloid plaques and tau tangles) and cerebral small-vessel disease (from hypertension and diabetes) each push the brain toward a clinical threshold; because their injury adds together, reducing the vascular load lowers the total burden and can keep a person below the line for longer.](/figures/mechanisms/dementia-two-pathways.svg){#fig-two-pathways}

The two rarely occur alone. In older people, **mixed pathology is the rule**: most brains at
post-mortem show more than one process. The classic demonstration is the Nun Study, where, among
people with the *same* amount of Alzheimer pathology, those who also had small strokes were far more
likely to have been visibly demented in life. The safe way to teach this is that the two kinds of
damage **add up**: vascular injury lowers the threshold at which neurodegeneration shows as dementia.
That has a hopeful corollary. We cannot yet clear plaques and tangles with food, but we *can* reduce
the vascular load, and doing so lowers the total burden whichever model turns out to be right.

## What's good for the heart is good for the brain

This is the chapter's central, evidence-based message. The same cardiometabolic risk factors you
already know as heart-disease risks — raised blood pressure, diabetes, high LDL cholesterol, obesity,
smoking, physical inactivity — are also the modifiable drivers of the vascular contribution to
dementia. Nutrition acts on the brain mostly *through* this vascular route, by improving those risk
factors.

You will meet a striking figure: up to **~45%** of dementia is associated with a set of modifiable
factors across a lifetime (the Lancet Commission) [@livingston2024lancet]. Read it carefully, because
it is easy to oversell. It is a **population-attributable estimate**: a theoretical ceiling that
assumes these factors are fully causal and could be eliminated from the whole population. It is **not**
a promise that one person who controls their risk factors cuts their own risk by 45%, and it is **not**
proof from a trial. The individual risk factors each raise risk only modestly, and reducing vascular
damage is **one of several** ways they are thought to act [@livingston2024lancet]. The large number
comes from adding many common exposures across a life, not from one dominant cause. Two points make it
usable: the biggest *individual* contributors are actually **hearing loss** and **education/social
factors**, not diet; and **midlife** is the key window, so the message is a long game, not a deathbed
one.

The best trial evidence that treating a vascular risk factor protects the brain comes from **SPRINT
MIND**: intensively lowering blood pressure significantly reduced mild cognitive impairment and the
combined measure of mild impairment or dementia [@sprintmind2019jama]. It did not, on its own, reduce
dementia (the trial was stopped early for its heart benefit, leaving it underpowered for that
harder endpoint). Even with that caveat, it is the strongest signal we have, and it points at the
vessels.

## Can a diet prevent dementia?

Here the evidence and the headlines part company, the kind of gap the *Evidence vs Hype* chapter
teaches you to weigh. The **MIND diet** (a blend of Mediterranean and DASH eating, emphasising leafy greens,
berries, nuts, wholegrains, fish and olive oil) was linked in observational cohorts to a large
reduction in Alzheimer's disease: roughly **half the risk** in the highest-adherence group
[@morris2015mindad]. Impressive, and widely repeated as fact.

Then the pattern was put to the test. A three-year randomised trial of the MIND diet, built
specifically to answer the prevention question, found **no cognitive benefit** over a comparison group
also nudged toward better eating [@barnes2023mindrct]. The cohort signal did not survive the trial. The
usual suspects explain the gap: people who eat well differ in a hundred other ways (confounding), early
undiagnosed disease can dull appetite and worsen diet (reverse causation), and three years may be too
short. Which impressive claims survive a randomised trial is rarely obvious in advance.

<div data-island="trial-check" data-src="/diagrams/trial-check.yml"></div>

So what do you tell a patient? A Mediterranean-style or MIND-style pattern is **safe, affordable, good
for the heart, and reasonable to recommend** — as a low-regret choice, not as a proven shield against
dementia. Multidomain programmes that combine diet, exercise, cognitive activity and vascular
monitoring have improved cognitive test scores in trials such as FINGER [@ngandu2015finger], but even
the best of them has **not shown a reduction in dementia itself**, and the benefit probably runs
largely through the vascular arm. Recommend the pattern; do not promise it prevents dementia.

## The supplement question

Sooner or later the patient asks about a pill. **No supplement is proven to prevent dementia, and
several carry a downside.** The usual bag runs as follows.

| Supplement | Claim / rationale | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| **B vitamins** | Lower homocysteine; a small study showed slower brain shrinkage on scans [@smith2010vitacog] | Large trials found **no benefit to memory or thinking** [@clarke2014bvitamin]; an imaging change is not a clinical benefit |
| **Fish-oil (omega-3) capsules** | Omega-3 for the brain | Trials show **no cognitive benefit** [@sydenham2012cochraneomega3]; eat fish as food — the capsule is not the food |
| **Vitamin E** | Antioxidant for prevention | No benefit, and at high doses linked to a small **increase in death** [@miller2005vitamine]; this one can harm |
| **Ginkgo biloba** | The classic "brain tonic" | A large trial found it **did not reduce dementia** at all [@dekosky2008gem]; can increase bleeding and interact with blood thinners |
| **Coconut oil or "MCT"** | Marketed for Alzheimer's | No credible trial evidence, and coconut oil is about 90% saturated fat, so it works against the vascular lever that actually matters |
| **"Medical foods"** | Marketed for memory | A regulatory category with a lower bar than a medicine, not evidence that the product works |

A useful rule of thumb: be sceptical when a claim rests on a blood or scan marker rather than on
memory itself, on a small pilot, or on an after-the-fact subgroup. And when a patient with a family
history asks about **genetic testing** (the *APOE* gene), the answer is that we do not test for or act
on it in ordinary prevention; if they remain worried, that is a reason to refer, not to start a
supplement.

## Beyond the plate

The worried-well patient deserves the whole picture, and some of the strongest levers are not
dietary. **Physical activity** is a headline recommendation for brain health, working partly through
the same vessels (the *Healthy Ageing* chapter builds the case for exercise in older adults). **Correcting hearing
and vision** matters more than most people expect: uncorrected hearing loss is one of the single
largest modifiable contributors to dementia risk, so "get your hearing and eyes checked" is concrete,
high-value advice. **Staying socially and mentally engaged**, and **treating depression and sleep
problems**, round out the picture. Build cognitive and social reserve early; it is part of why rising
education levels are lowering dementia rates across generations.

## When the problem inverts: eating in advanced dementia

Everything above is prevention. Late in the disease the nutrition problem turns upside down. As
dementia advances, **weight loss and difficulty eating are expected**, driven by the disease itself:
most people with advanced dementia develop an eating problem, and it marks a late stage of the
illness [@mitchell2009cascade]. The instinct, and the family's plea, is to "do something so she
doesn't starve", and the ward reflex is to consider a feeding tube.

Here the evidence is unusually clear and unusually important. In advanced dementia, tube feeding
**does not prolong survival, prevent aspiration pneumonia, heal or prevent pressure sores, or improve
comfort**, and it can cause harm, including *more* pressure ulcers [@davies2021cochranetubefeeding;
@teno2012pressureulcers]. Every major body — geriatrics societies and the Choosing Wisely campaign —
converges on the same position: **careful hand feeding, offering food for comfort, is preferred**
[@ags2014feedingtubes]. The skill for the generalist is to recognise the trajectory, explain gently
that the weight loss is part of the illness rather than neglect, and **refer for a goals-of-care
conversation** rather than reach for a tube. (Two things are separate and still appropriate: correcting
a genuine vitamin deficiency, and supporting oral intake with feeding help earlier in the disease. A
common ward reflex to watch is routinely thickening fluids for swallowing problems; the evidence for
it is weak, so recognise it and refer to speech therapy rather than assume.) The recognition-and-refer
posture, and the malnutrition screening behind it, are the *Undernutrition & Malnutrition* chapter's.

## The Singapore picture

Singapore is ageing quickly, and dementia is common: about **1 in 11 residents aged 60 and over** live
with it, and about **half of community dementia is still undiagnosed** [@subramaniam2025wisedecade].
That undiagnosed half is the argument for the generalist's recognise-and-refer role. Risk maps onto
the vascular story locally too: dementia is commoner in Indian than Chinese residents, mirroring the
higher burden of diabetes and vascular disease, so "brain health is vascular health" is a message that
fits national diabetes and hypertension efforts.

Two local realities shape counselling. First, there is a large market of over-the-counter "brain
tonics" — ginkgo, fish oil, and traditional tonics — and being sold in a pharmacy means neither proven
nor approved for dementia. Second, the socio-cultural signals are worth knowing: overall **diet
quality** tracks with better cognition in a multi-ethnic Singapore cohort [@huangx2023singaporemec],
and, intriguingly, regular **curry (curcumin)** intake has been associated with lower rates of new
cognitive impairment in older Singaporeans [@lu2025curry]. Treat the curry finding as a
hypothesis-generating local curiosity, not a prescription. Local prevention programmes are adapting
the multidomain approach to Singaporean food and life, which is exactly the "translate the evidence to
the local plate" move this book keeps making.

## What to tell a patient worried about dementia

Start by taking the fear seriously, then lead with what actually works: look after the blood vessels,
because what is good for the heart is good for the brain. Offer a Mediterranean-style pattern as a
sensible, low-regret choice while being clear it is not a proven cure, and steer away from the
supplements, which cost money and can occasionally harm. Add the non-food levers that carry real
weight, especially activity and getting hearing and vision corrected. Later, if the same family
returns with a relative who is losing weight in advanced dementia, the kind, evidence-based answer is
comfort, not a tube, with a referral for a proper conversation about goals. Recognise the levers,
counsel truthfully, and know when to hand over.

## Check your understanding

<div data-island="quiz" data-src="/quizzes/brain-health-dementia.quiz.yml"></div>

## Practise: "what should I eat so I don't get dementia?"

A 52-year-old whose mother has dementia asks you what diet will prevent it. Her blood pressure is up
and her fasting glucose is in the prediabetes range, and she has read that the MIND diet halves
Alzheimer's risk and wants a supplement recommendation. Counsel her truthfully, without selling false
hope. Your choices branch the case.

::: {.callout-tip}
Progress is saved locally — replay to explore the other paths.
:::

<div data-island="case" data-src="/cases/dementia-prevention.case.yml"></div>

## Practise: the feeding-tube request

Mrs Koh's mother, 84, has advanced dementia and is eating less and losing weight. The family, upset,
asks the team to insert a feeding tube "so she doesn't starve". Decide what the evidence supports and
how to hold this conversation.

<div data-island="case" data-src="/cases/dementia-feeding.case.yml"></div>
